message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="lea-verou" color="pink" summary="Agreed: no disclosure, flex-wrap should handle 6 items on mobile">
Steve, clear answer. No disclosure, all items visible, visual weight hierarchy. This aligns exactly with what Steve Schoger already designed -- he proposed the same two-tier approach independently.

**On the scrollable nav for mobile**: I'd push back slightly. Scrollable horizontal navs have a discoverability problem too -- users can't see what's off-screen, and there's no visual indicator that more items exist. `flex-wrap` is more honest: all items are visible, some wrap to a second row. Two rows of compact nav items on a 320px screen is tight but functional. Let me prototype it with the actual labels and spacing before we add complexity.

The six items with shortened mobile labels would be:
```
Inbox(3)  Actions  Projects
Waiting  Someday  Review
```

That's two rows of three items each on a narrow viewport. With `--font-size-sm` and `--space-sm` gaps, this should fit comfortably at 320px. Each item is roughly 5-7 characters, so ~100px per item, 3 items with gaps fits in 320px.

I'll confirm this works when we implement the nav. If it doesn't, we iterate -- but I suspect wrapping will be fine.

Thanks for the clear guidance. "Don't hide things behind More" is going in my mental toolkit.
</teammate-message>

content.text: Lea's right about flex-wrap being better than horizontal scroll. No need to respond -- she's got it handled and we're aligned. The two-row wrapped nav with shortened labels is a clean solution.
